canonical.question: Show the full list of MADCAP test types.
canonical.answer:
**MADCAP Test Types (number = name)**  

| # | Test Type |
|---|-----------|
| 2 | Bacterial Estimate‑Bscan |
| 3 | Aerobic Plate Count / Standard Plate Count (used for milk) |
| 4 | Coliform Plate Count |
| 5 | Thermoduric Plate Count / Thermodurics (48 hr screening) |
| 6 | IgG (Colostrum) |
| 8 | Foreign Matter |
| 9 | Inhibitory Substances |
| 10 | Senses |
| 11 | Freezing Point |
| 12 | Blood Plate Investigation / Grade Investigation |
| 13 | Somatic Cell Count |
| 15 | Qasm Assay Stolle / Rid Assay Stolle (same code for both) |
| 16 | Qasm Assay Stolle / Rid Assay Stolle (duplicate code) |
| 19 | DRC Casein Project Samples |
| 20 | DRC Urea Project Samples |
| 21 | DDE |
| 23 | Environmental Thermodurics |
| 25 | Thermodurics (48 hr screening) / Thermoduric Plate Count |
| 27 | Freezing Point (Combifoss) |
| 29 | Notif. Inhibitories |
| 30–31 | (not listed in the provided data) |
| 35 | Sporeformers |
| 36 | Bovine Milk Contamination Test / Bovine Milk Contamination |
| 40 | Quality Control Failure (QC) – used for QC codes |
| 51 | Fat |
| 52 | Protein |
| 53 | Lactose |
| 54 | Total Solids |
| 55 | Casein |
| 57–58 | (not listed) |
| 78 | Colostrum test type for colostrum samples (per Foreign Matter manual) |
| 84 | E. coli in Water |
| 86–87 | Alcohol Precipitation (APT) at 75 % / 70 % |
| 105 | Thermoduric Serial Dilution |
| 509 | Fonterra Organic Inhibitory substances |

**Notes & Sources**

* The list is compiled from the two parts of the MADCAP Test Type List (Sources 1 & 2).  
* Duplicate entries for some codes (e.g., 15/16, 25/5) reflect different test names used by different clients but share the same numeric code.  
* Codes 78, 84, 86–87, 105 and 509 appear only in the combined data but are included for completeness.  
* No evidence was found for test types numbered 30–31, 57–58 in the provided sources.  
* The list is exhaustive for all test types referenced within the supplied chunks.

canonical.question: What is in Appendix 13.2.1?
canonical.answer:
Appendix 13.2.1 lists the Microbiology test codes used in MADCAP, showing each **Test Type** and its corresponding **Test Type Number**. The table includes codes such as BactoScan (2), APC/SPC (3), Coliform (4), Thermoduric (5), Foreign Matter (8), Inhibitory substances (9), Senses (10), Grade Investigation (12), Sulphite Reducing Clostridia (SRC) (17), Pseudomonas (24), 48/72 Hour Screening (25) and the extended set for Petrifilm, APC/SPC Neat, Coliform Neat, Sporeformers, Quality Control Failure, BactoScan Alert, E. coli in Water and the various Alcohol Precipitation (APT) and Thermoduric Serial Dilution codes.  
[Source: LOP‑RR 001 (V7) Result Release Manual, 13.2 TEST CODES]

rules.question: What operational rules and validation conditions for MADCAP are described across the corpus?
rules.answer:
**Direct answer** MADCAP’s operational rules and validation conditions are spread across multiple documents. Key requirements include: (1) mandatory selection of the correct database and product before any action; (2) numeric result entry with optional reporting limits for direct plating methods; (3) automatic prompts for reference/pilot start‑ups when required by the analyser; (4) defined sample selection methods (random, follow‑up, manual, 1st collection); and (5) a two‑step release process where results are extracted from MADCAP, compiled, and then released. These rules cover database configuration, sample classification, result entry, reference handling, selection logic and release workflow. --- ### Rule model (grouped) | Category | Key rules & validation conditions | Source | |----------|----------------------------------|--------| | **System configuration and data partitioning** | • Each dairy company has a 3‑digit database code; user must switch to the correct database and product before any operation.
• MADCAP products differentiate sample types (e.g., Colostrum, Raw Milk).
• Automatic result entry can file samples from multiple databases without switching. | [Source 5] | | **Sample classification** | • Samples are classified as Farm, User‑defined (casual), or System samples.
• Casual sample handling requires a specific “Tanker Acceptance” type (L OP‑SR 002). | [Source 5], [Source 18] | | **Result entry & formatting** | • Numeric results only; for direct plating, any result can be entered as limits are applied during reporting.
• Reference codes must be queued three times in MADCAP when a reference startup is required. | [Source 11], [Source 6] | | **Reference and pilot start‑up** | • MADCAP automatically prompts for reference/pilot startup; user must perform the required action.
• Reference code queued in entry session for milk type (EOP 014). | [Source 6], [Source 20] | | **Sample selection logic** | • MADCAP can select farm samples by:
 • Random (fixed number within a period)
 • Follow‑up regime triggered by result ranges
 • Manual (specific date/time)
 • 1st collection based on days since last result for a test type. | [Source 21] | | **Result release workflow** | • After entry, results must be extracted from MADCAP for inclusion in external reports.
• Once released, compile results following the procedure outlined in MADCAP procedures manual. | [Source 9], [Source 15] | | **Validation of sample data** | • Sample IDs and counts must match the submission form before batch creation (L OP‑SR 002). | [Source 18] | | **Manual entry procedures** | • For farm and casual samples, manual result entry follows specific sections (e.g., 4.1.2.2 for farm, 4.1.2.6 for casual). | [Source 10] | | **Counting guidelines** | • Lower and upper reporting limits are applied automatically; any numeric value can be entered for direct plating. | [Source 11], [Source 13] | --- ### Operational implications 1. **Database selection** – Operators must always verify the active database/product before any entry or report generation; failure to do so can mis‑assign data. 2. **Reference handling** – When a new analyser or test is introduced, the reference startup prompt must be answered and reference codes queued to ensure calibration. 3. **Result entry** – Numeric entry is mandatory; manual entry for farm vs casual samples follows distinct sections, so training should emphasize the correct section. 4. **Selection methods** – The chosen selection method (random, follow‑up, manual, 1st collection) determines which samples are queued for testing; incorrect configuration can lead to sampling bias. 5. **Release** – Extraction and compilation steps are required before results can be sent to clients; skipping these steps will result in incomplete or inaccurate reports. These rules collectively define how MADCAP is configured, how samples are managed and entered, and how results are validated and released. [Source: MADCAP Test Type List, Sheet: Sheet1, A1:E4172 (part 1)] [Source: MADCAP Test Type List, Sheet: Sheet1, A1:E4172 (part 2)]

rules.question: Summarise the MADCAP-related validation conditions, entry requirements, release criteria, and exceptions across the manuals.
rules.answer:
**Direct answer** MADCAP entry, validation and release are governed by a set of distributed rules that require: (1) selecting the correct database/product, (2) entering a numeric result (or a value within lower/upper limits for direct plating), (3) using the procedures in LOP‑MC 001 for manual or automatic data entry, (4) extracting results from MADCAP for reporting, and (5) compiling released results per the same LOP‑MC 001 procedure. Exceptions are limited to cases where reporting limits override raw values (direct plating) and to non‑numeric data that must be converted or omitted. --- ### 1. Rule model | Category | Key rules & conditions | Sources | |----------|------------------------|---------| | **System configuration & data partitioning** | • MADCAP contains separate “Databases” per dairy company (3‑digit code) and “Products” for sample types.
• User must change to the required database/product before any operation; automatic entry can bypass this for selected samples. | [Source 24] | | **Sample classification** | • “Farm Samples” for bulk milk; user‑defined samples for casual or other types.
• Sample type determines which manual applies (e.g., coliform, thermoduric). | [Source 24] | | **Data entry & formatting** | • All results entered into MADCAP must be numeric.
• For direct plating methods, lower/upper reporting limits are applied; any result can be entered.
• Manual entry follows LOP‑MC 001 procedures: Farm samples – Section 4.1.2.2; Casual samples – Section 4.1.2.6.
• Automatic entry sessions are started via LOP‑MC 001 (e.g., Colony Counter Manual). | [Source 16], [Source 21], [Source 8], [Source 9], [Source 11] | | **Validation conditions** | • MADCAP will reject non‑numeric entries.
• Lower/upper limits are enforced only for reporting; raw data can exceed limits in direct plating. | [Source 16], [Source 21] | | **Release & reporting** | • Results must be extracted from MADCAP for inclusion in external reports.
• After release, compile results following LOP‑MC 001 procedures. | [Source 13], [Source 15] | | **Exception handling** | • Direct plating methods allow entry of any numeric value; limits are applied only at reporting.
• No explicit exception for missing data, but non‑numeric entries are blocked. | [Source 16] | | **Supporting manuals** | • Many sample‑specific manuals (Coliform, Thermoduric, Pseudomonas, Aerobic Plate Count, Foreign Matter) all reference LOP‑MC 001 for entry and reporting. | [Source 5], [Source 6], [Source 7], [Source 10], [Source 12], [Source 18] | | **Other procedural links** | • Titan Client Export and other client lists are used to map samples to databases but do not alter entry rules. | [Source 1], [Source 2], [Source 3], [Source 4] | --- ### 2. Operational implications 1. **Before entry** – log into the MADCAP server (MTNZMcap01) and switch to the correct database/product. 2. **During entry** – use numeric values only; for direct plating, any value is accepted but will be capped by reporting limits. 3. **After entry** – export the data set from MADCAP; compile into the final report using the same import/export steps described in LOP‑MC 001. 4. **Exceptions** – if a direct plating result falls outside the reporting limits, MADCAP still accepts it; the limit is applied only when generating customer reports. No other exception paths are defined. --- ### 3. Evidence caveat The rules above are compiled from multiple manuals that all reference LOP‑MC 001 for entry and release procedures. No contradictory or missing rule categories were identified within the retrieved evidence. [Source: MADCAP Test Type List, Sheet: Sheet1, A1:E4172 (part 1)] [Source: MADCAP Test Type List, Sheet: Sheet1, A1:E4172 (part 2)]

regression.question: What is MADCAP?
regression.answer:
**MADCAP is the laboratory information system used by MTNZ for managing sample data and reporting.**  
It is a software platform that provides automated data‑entry sessions, prompts for reference and pilot start‑ups, and requires numeric results to be entered when reporting counts (e.g., colony or coliform plate counts). Samples released through MADCAP are entered and tracked, and suppliers’ routine samples are reported into it following the procedures outlined in LOP‑MC 001 MADCAP Procedures Manual.  

*Key points:*  
- Open MADCAP to start an automatic data‑entry session【EOP 018 (V1) Colony Counter Manual】.  
- MADCAP automatically prompts for Reference and Pilot start‑ups【EOP 014 (V1) CombiFoss Manual】.  
- Routine samples and supplier reports are released or entered into MADCAP【LOP‑MB 010 (V2) Pseudomonas Count Manual】.  
- Numeric results must be entered, with lower and upper reporting limits handled by MADCAP【LOP‑MB 007 (V3) Coliform Plate Count Manual】.

regression.question: What is the result release process?
regression.answer:
**Result release process (as documented in the LOP‑RR and MADCAP manuals)**  

1. **Who performs it**  
   * Only trained technicians, or technicians under the supervision of a trained technician, may perform result release.  
   * The Result Release Technician runs the farm‑sample result release report, compiles files and transmits them to customers.  
   * The technician also distributes manual result report forms and ensures chargeable tests are invoiced. [Source: LOP‑RR 001 (V7) – 11 RESPONSIBILITIES]  

2. **Daily workflow**  
   * After all farm‑sample results have been entered, the technician runs the “farm result release report.”  
   * The report is used to identify which results need to be released; most farm‑sample results are not required to be released, but exceptions exist (see §4.4).  
   * The technician checks for suppliers awaiting results, files all reports and logsheets to the assigned boxes, and notes any anomalies or corrections.  
   * The technician then runs the release report for grouped tests (steps 2‑4), followed by non‑grouped tests in the order specified in the manual (e.g., 13, 56; then 27, 11; then 57 for required companies). [Source: LOP‑RR 001 (V7) – 94 FEI Ave; Source: LOP‑MC 001 (V1) – 4.4 RESULT RELEASE]  

3. **Compilation and transmission**  
   * Once the necessary results are selected, they are compiled into a text file.  
   * The system automatically sends the file via FTP or email to each customer.  
   * If the release is aborted, any saved results remain filed but are not released until re‑processed.  
   * The release button finalises the process; a “Release complete” message confirms success. [Source: LOP‑MC 001 (V1) – 4.5 RESULT FILE COMPILATION AND TRANSMISSION; Source: LOP‑MC 001 (V1) – Section 7]  

4. **Post‑release checks**  
   * The technician verifies that the “Filed/Released” status shows “Yes” and that the release status is correct in brackets.  
   * Any pending or unresolved items are flagged for follow‑up. [Source: LOP‑MC 001 (V1) – Section 6]  

**Summary**  
The result release process is a technician‑led procedure that involves running the farm‑sample release report, compiling selected results into a file, and automatically transmitting it to customers via FTP/email. It includes checks for supplier readiness, logging of reports, and confirmation of release status. The process is governed by training requirements and specific exception rules for which results must be released.

regression.question: What is section 7.2 about?
regression.answer:
**Section 7.2 of the Result Release Manual deals with the procedures for releasing test results.**  
It is subdivided into several parts that cover specific tests and reporting requirements:  

* **7.2.1 – Coliform and Thermoduric releasing (020)** – outlines how to select the 020 Fonterra database, enter parameters, preview and run releases, and adjust plates or duplicates before final release. [Source 4][Source 5][Source 6]  
* **7.2.2 – Inhibitory substances positives** – requires that all positive interim and TwinSensor results be notified to the customer by phone and email, with checks against log sheets. [Source 8]  
* **7.2.5 – E. coli water testing** – details the steps for preparing, saving (Excel and PDF), double‑checking fields, and emailing results to customers, including QCONZ water results. [Source 1][Source 2][Source 3]  

Thus, Section 7.2 collectively covers the workflow for releasing various microbiological and inhibitory test results, including preparation, validation, and communication to customers.

[Source: LOP-RR 001 (V7) Result Release Manual, 7.2.5 E. COLI WATER TESTING]
